It is located in [[Tajima Province]]. == instance of (P31) == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|lt=Shinto shrine|ar|جينجا|arz|ضريح شنتو|bcl|Shinto shrine|ca|Jinja|cs|Šintoistická {{wikidata link|Q11368920}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=Kukuhi Shrine |画像=[[ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Honden Shintoism002.JPG|270px]]
Haiden |所在地={{jalink|Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290}}{{jalink|Toyooka|Q696388}}Shimonomiya 318-2 |位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神={{jalink|Kukunochi-no-Kami}} |創建=Unknown (reign of {{jalink|Emperor Suinin|Q314850}}) |社格={{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small)
former {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}} |例祭={{jalink|October 15|Q2919}} |神事= |本殿=Three-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |地図= {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Kukuhi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Kukuhi-jinja 04230.JPG|thumb|250px|Honden (Important Cultural Property)]] '''Kukuhi Shrine''' (くくひじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Shimonomiya, {{jalink|Toyooka|Q696388}}, {{jalink|Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290}}. It is a {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Shikinai small shrine}}, and its former rank under the {{jalink|modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|shrine ranking system}} was village shrine. === Enshrined deity === * {{jalink|Kukunochi-no-Kami}} (くくのちのかみ) === History === The {{jalink|honden|Q2044994}} is a three-bay nagare-zukuri structure with a kokera-buki roof. It was built in {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193577}} 4 ({{jalink|1507|Q6161}}) in the {{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845}}, and is designated a national {{jalink|Important Cultural Property|Q1188622}}. Edo-period records describe it as an old shrine also called Munakata Daimyōjin, and there is also a theory that it enshrined {{jalink|Tagiribime-no-Mikoto}}. === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Property (national designation) === * Honden - three-bay nagare-zukuri, kokera-buki. Built in Eishō 4 (1507), Muromachi period. === Stork legend === According to the {{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}}, on the first day of the tenth month in the 23rd year of Emperor {{jalink|Suinin|Q314850}}, while the emperor stood before the palace with Prince '''Homutsuwake''' (誉津別皇子), a '''kugui''' (an old word for {{jalink|stork|Q998495}}) cried as it crossed the sky. When the prince asked, "What is that?", the emperor rejoiced greatly and asked his retainers who could capture the bird. '''{{jalink|Amenoyukawa no Tana|Q52319907}}''' said, "I will surely capture and present it," and pursued the great bird through many provinces; it is said he captured it in {{jalink|Izumo Province|Q582846}}, or according to another tradition, in {{jalink|Tajima Province|Q1053727}}. On the first day of the eleventh month, he successfully presented the bird. At that time the prince was thirty years old, but had still not spoken and only made sounds like a crying child; on that day he spoke human words for the first time. Because the kugui was such a sacred bird, its nesting place was called Kukuhi. Later, a shrine was built there and the tree deity '''Kukunochi-no-Kami''' was enshrined. This is said to be the beginning of Kukuhi Shrine. At that time, however, the '''Toyooka Basin''' was called "Kimesaki no Umi" and was entirely an inlet of the sea; Shimonomiya stood on the shoreline of that inlet. The area was also densely wooded, a place where {{jalink|kodama|Q1515938}} were thought to dwell, and a scenic site where kami themselves were believed to reside. It is therefore natural that earlier people perceived the mystery and sacredness of this nature, worshipped the tree deity Kukunochi-no-Kami, and revered the great divine virtue of trees. === Amulets === Amulets designed with storks (red, green, and purple) are well known as charms for fertility and safe childbirth (based on the tradition that storks bring children). In the fallow season (mainly September to May), storks fly in to the adjacent wetland. ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Torii001.jpg|Approach entrance (stork images are painted on the railings of the sacred bridge over the Shimonomiya River) ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Sintoism002.jpg|Wari-haiden ファイル:Kukuhi-jinja_0383.JPG|Stork-designed amulets === Access === * About 10 minutes on foot from {{jalink|KTR}}{{jalink|Kōnotori-no-Sato Station|Q7676863}}. * From {{jalink|San'in Main Line|Q1135170}}{{jalink|Toyooka Station (Hyogo)|Q871612|Toyooka Station}}, take {{jalink|Zentan Bus|Q11389110}} bound for Shimonomiya and get off at "Shimonomiya-guchi". === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Related items === === External links === {{Commonscat|Kukuhi-jinja}} {{Osm box|n|5597854784}} * [http://www.city.toyooka.lg.jp/kanko/miru/1002163.html Facility Guide: Kukuhi Shrine] - Toyooka City {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くくひしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:兵庫県の神社]] [[Category:但馬国の式内社]] [[Category:兵庫県の旧村社]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]] [[Category:兵庫県の重要文化財]] [[Category:室町時代の建築]] [[Category:豊岡市の建築物]] [[Category:豊岡市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qqq]]

{{wikidata link|Q11368920}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=Kukuhi Shrine |画像=[[ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Honden Shintoism002.JPG|270px]]
Haiden |所在地={{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388}}Shimonomiya 318-2 |位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kukunochi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN}} |創建=Unknown (reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850}}) |社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} |例祭={{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919}} |神事= |本殿=Three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |地図= {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Kukuhi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Kukuhi-jinja 04230.JPG|thumb|250px|Honden (Important Cultural Property)]] '''Kukuhi Shrine''' (くくひじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in Shimonomiya, {{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388}}, {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinai small shrine}}, and its former rank under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine ranking system}} was village shrine. === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kukunochi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN}} (くくのちのかみ) === History === The {{ill|lt=honden|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994}} is a three-bay nagare-zukuri structure with a kokera-buki roof. It was built in {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1507|Q6161|WD=Q6161}}) in the {{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|WD=Q334845}}, and is designated a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}}. Edo-period records describe it as an old shrine also called Munakata Daimyōjin, and there is also a theory that it enshrined {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tagiribime-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}. === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Property (national designation) === * Honden - three-bay nagare-zukuri, kokera-buki. Built in Eishō 4 (1507), Muromachi period. === Stork legend === According to the {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935}}, on the first day of the tenth month in the 23rd year of Emperor {{ill|lt=Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850}}, while the emperor stood before the palace with Prince '''Homutsuwake''' (誉津別皇子), a '''kugui''' (an old word for {{ill|lt=stork|Q998495|WD=Q998495}}) cried as it crossed the sky. When the prince asked, "What is that?", the emperor rejoiced greatly and asked his retainers who could capture the bird. '''{{ill|lt=Amenoyukawa no Tana|Q52319907|WD=Q52319907}}''' said, "I will surely capture and present it," and pursued the great bird through many provinces; it is said he captured it in {{ill|lt=Izumo Province|Q582846|WD=Q582846}}, or according to another tradition, in {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727}}. On the first day of the eleventh month, he successfully presented the bird. At that time the prince was thirty years old, but had still not spoken and only made sounds like a crying child; on that day he spoke human words for the first time. Because the kugui was such a sacred bird, its nesting place was called Kukuhi. Later, a shrine was built there and the tree deity '''Kukunochi-no-Kami''' was enshrined. This is said to be the beginning of Kukuhi Shrine. At that time, however, the '''Toyooka Basin''' was called "Kimesaki no Umi" and was entirely an inlet of the sea; Shimonomiya stood on the shoreline of that inlet. The area was also densely wooded, a place where {{ill|lt=kodama|Q1515938|WD=Q1515938}} were thought to dwell, and a scenic site where kami themselves were believed to reside. It is therefore natural that earlier people perceived the mystery and sacredness of this nature, worshipped the tree deity Kukunochi-no-Kami, and revered the great divine virtue of trees. === Amulets === Amulets designed with storks (red, green, and purple) are well known as charms for fertility and safe childbirth (based on the tradition that storks bring children). In the fallow season (mainly September to May), storks fly in to the adjacent wetland. ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Torii001.jpg|Approach entrance (stork images are painted on the railings of the sacred bridge over the Shimonomiya River) ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Sintoism002.jpg|Wari-haiden ファイル:Kukuhi-jinja_0383.JPG|Stork-designed amulets === Access === * About 10 minutes on foot from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=KTR|UNKNOWN}}{{ill|lt=Kōnotori-no-Sato Station|Q7676863|WD=Q7676863}}. * From {{ill|lt=San'in Main Line|Q1135170|WD=Q1135170}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka Station (Hyogo)|Q871612|WD=Q871612|lt=Toyooka Station}}, take {{ill|lt=Zentan Bus|Q11389110|WD=Q11389110}} bound for Shimonomiya and get off at "Shimonomiya-guchi". === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Related items === === External links === {{Commonscat|Kukuhi-jinja}} {{Osm box|n|5597854784}} * [http://www.city.toyooka.lg.jp/kanko/miru/1002163.html Facility Guide: Kukuhi Shrine] - Toyooka City {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くくひしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:兵庫県の神社]] [[Category:但馬国の式内社]] [[Category:兵庫県の旧村社]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]] [[Category:兵庫県の重要文化財]] [[Category:室町時代の建築]] [[Category:豊岡市の建築物]] [[Category:豊岡市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qqq]]

In the fallow season (mainly September to May), storks fly in to the adjacent wetland. ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Torii001.jpg|Approach entrance (stork images are painted on the railings of the sacred bridge over the Shimonomiya River) ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Sintoism002.jpg|Wari-haiden ファイル:Kukuhi-jinja_0383.JPG|Stork-designed amulets === Access === * About 10 minutes on foot from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=KTR|1=UNKNOWN}}{{ill|lt=Kōnotori-no-Sato Station|Q7676863|WD=Q7676863}}. * From {{ill|lt=San'in Main Line|Q1135170|WD=Q1135170}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka Station (Hyogo)|Q871612|WD=Q871612|lt=Toyooka Station}}, take {{ill|lt=Zentan Bus|Q11389110|WD=Q11389110}} bound for Shimonomiya and get off at "Shimonomiya-guchi". === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Related items === === External links === {{Commonscat|Kukuhi-jinja}} {{Osm box|n|5597854784}} * [http://www.city.toyooka.lg.jp/kanko/miru/1002163.html Facility Guide: Kukuhi Shrine] - Toyooka City {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くくひしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:兵庫県の神社]] [[Category:但馬国の式内社]] [[Category:兵庫県の旧村社]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]] [[Category:兵庫県の重要文化財]] [[Category:室町時代の建築]] [[Category:豊岡市の建築物]] [[Category:豊岡市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qqq]]

Haiden |所在地={{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388}}Shimonomiya 318-2 |位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kukunochi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} |創建=Unknown (reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850}}) |社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} |例祭={{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919}} |神事= |本殿=Three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |地図= {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Kukuhi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Kukuhi-jinja 04230.JPG|thumb|250px|Honden (Important Cultural Property)]] '''Kukuhi Shrine''' (くくひじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in Shimonomiya, {{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388}}, {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinai small shrine}}, and its former rank under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine ranking system}} was village shrine. === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kukunochi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} (くくのちのかみ) === History === The {{ill|lt=honden|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994}} is a three-bay nagare-zukuri structure with a kokera-buki roof. It was built in {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1507|Q6161|WD=Q6161}}) in the {{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|WD=Q334845}}, and is designated a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}}. Edo-period records describe it as an old shrine also called Munakata Daimyōjin, and there is also a theory that it enshrined {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tagiribime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Property (national designation) === * Honden - three-bay nagare-zukuri, kokera-buki. Built in Eishō 4 (1507), Muromachi period. === Stork legend === According to the {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935}}, on the first day of the tenth month in the 23rd year of Emperor {{ill|lt=Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850}}, while the emperor stood before the palace with Prince '''Homutsuwake''' (誉津別皇子), a '''kugui''' (an old word for {{ill|lt=stork|Q998495|WD=Q998495}}) cried as it crossed the sky. When the prince asked, "What is that?", the emperor rejoiced greatly and asked his retainers who could capture the bird. '''{{ill|lt=Amenoyukawa no Tana|Q52319907|WD=Q52319907}}''' said, "I will surely capture and present it," and pursued the great bird through many provinces; it is said he captured it in {{ill|lt=Izumo Province|Q582846|WD=Q582846}}, or according to another tradition, in {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727}}. On the first day of the eleventh month, he successfully presented the bird. At that time the prince was thirty years old, but had still not spoken and only made sounds like a crying child; on that day he spoke human words for the first time. Because the kugui was such a sacred bird, its nesting place was called Kukuhi. Later, a shrine was built there and the tree deity '''Kukunochi-no-Kami''' was enshrined. This is said to be the beginning of Kukuhi Shrine. At that time, however, the '''Toyooka Basin''' was called "Kimesaki no Umi" and was entirely an inlet of the sea; Shimonomiya stood on the shoreline of that inlet. The area was also densely wooded, a place where {{ill|lt=kodama|Q1515938|WD=Q1515938}} were thought to dwell, and a scenic site where kami themselves were believed to reside. It is therefore natural that earlier people perceived the mystery and sacredness of this nature, worshipped the tree deity Kukunochi-no-Kami, and revered the great divine virtue of trees. === Amulets === Amulets designed with storks (red, green, and purple) are well known as charms for fertility and safe childbirth (based on the tradition that storks bring children). In the fallow season (mainly September to May), storks fly in to the adjacent wetland. ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Torii001.jpg|Approach entrance (stork images are painted on the railings of the sacred bridge over the Shimonomiya River) ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Sintoism002.jpg|Wari-haiden ファイル:Kukuhi-jinja_0383.JPG|Stork-designed amulets === Access === * About 10 minutes on foot from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=KTR|1=UNKNOWN}}{{ill|lt=Kōnotori-no-Sato Station|Q7676863|WD=Q7676863}}. * From {{ill|lt=San'in Main Line|Q1135170|WD=Q1135170}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka Station (Hyogo)|Q871612|WD=Q871612|lt=Toyooka Station}}, take {{ill|lt=Zentan Bus|Q11389110|WD=Q11389110}} bound for Shimonomiya and get off at "Shimonomiya-guchi". === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Related items === === External links === {{Commonscat|Kukuhi-jinja}} {{Osm box|n|5597854784}} * [http://www.city.toyooka.lg.jp/kanko/miru/1002163.html Facility Guide: Kukuhi Shrine] - Toyooka City {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くくひしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:兵庫県の神社]] [[Category:但馬国の式内社]] [[Category:兵庫県の旧村社]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]] [[Category:兵庫県の重要文化財]] [[Category:室町時代の建築]] [[Category:豊岡市の建築物]] [[Category:豊岡市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qqq]]

In the fallow season (mainly September to May), storks fly in to the adjacent wetland. ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Torii001.jpg|Approach entrance (stork images are painted on the railings of the sacred bridge over the Shimonomiya River) ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Sintoism002.jpg|Wari-haiden ファイル:Kukuhi-jinja_0383.JPG|Stork-designed amulets === Access === * About 10 minutes on foot from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=KTR|1=UNKNOWN}}{{ill|lt=Kōnotori-no-Sato Station|Q7676863|WD=Q7676863}}. * From {{ill|lt=San'in Main Line|Q1135170|WD=Q1135170}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka Station (Hyogo)|Q871612|WD=Q871612|lt=Toyooka Station}}, take {{ill|lt=Zentan Bus|Q11389110|WD=Q11389110}} bound for Shimonomiya and get off at "Shimonomiya-guchi". === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Related items === === External links === {{Commonscat|Kukuhi-jinja}} {{Osm box|n|5597854784}} * [http://www.city.toyooka.lg.jp/kanko/miru/1002163.html Facility Guide: Kukuhi Shrine] - Toyooka City {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くくひしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:兵庫県の神社]] [[Category:但馬国の式内社]] [[Category:兵庫県の旧村社]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]] [[Category:兵庫県の重要文化財]] [[Category:室町時代の建築]] [[Category:豊岡市の建築物]] [[Category:豊岡市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qqq]]

category|Category:Kukuhi-jinja}} [[JA:久久比神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11368920}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=Kukuhi Shrine |画像=[[ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Honden Shintoism002.JPG|270px]]
Haiden |所在地={{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}Shimonomiya 318-2 |位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kukunochi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} |創建=Unknown (reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}) |社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |例祭={{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}} |神事= |本殿=Three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |地図= {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Kukuhi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Kukuhi-jinja 04230.JPG|thumb|250px|Honden (Important Cultural Property)]] '''Kukuhi Shrine''' (くくひじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Shimonomiya, {{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinai small shrine|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former rank under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was village shrine. === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kukunochi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} (くくのちのかみ) === History === The {{ill|lt=honden|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}} is a three-bay nagare-zukuri structure with a kokera-buki roof. It was built in {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1507|Q6161|WD=Q6161|1=1507}}) in the {{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|WD=Q334845|1=Muromachi period}}, and is designated a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}. Edo-period records describe it as an old shrine also called Munakata Daimyōjin, and there is also a theory that it enshrined {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tagiribime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Property (national designation) === * Honden - three-bay nagare-zukuri, kokera-buki. Built in Eishō 4 (1507), Muromachi period. === Stork legend === According to the {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}, on the first day of the tenth month in the 23rd year of Emperor {{ill|lt=Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}, while the emperor stood before the palace with Prince '''Homutsuwake''' (誉津別皇子), a '''kugui''' (an old word for {{ill|lt=stork|Q998495|WD=Q998495|1=Oriental stork}}) cried as it crossed the sky. When the prince asked, "What is that?", the emperor rejoiced greatly and asked his retainers who could capture the bird. '''{{ill|lt=Amenoyukawa no Tana|Q52319907|WD=Q52319907|1=Ame-no-Yukawata}}''' said, "I will surely capture and present it," and pursued the great bird through many provinces; it is said he captured it in {{ill|lt=Izumo Province|Q582846|WD=Q582846|1=Izumo Province}}, or according to another tradition, in {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727|1=Tajima Province}}. On the first day of the eleventh month, he successfully presented the bird. At that time the prince was thirty years old, but had still not spoken and only made sounds like a crying child; on that day he spoke human words for the first time. Because the kugui was such a sacred bird, its nesting place was called Kukuhi. Later, a shrine was built there and the tree deity '''Kukunochi-no-Kami''' was enshrined. This is said to be the beginning of Kukuhi Shrine. At that time, however, the '''Toyooka Basin''' was called "Kimesaki no Umi" and was entirely an inlet of the sea; Shimonomiya stood on the shoreline of that inlet. The area was also densely wooded, a place where {{ill|lt=kodama|Q1515938|WD=Q1515938|1=Kodama (spirit)}} were thought to dwell, and a scenic site where kami themselves were believed to reside. It is therefore natural that earlier people perceived the mystery and sacredness of this nature, worshipped the tree deity Kukunochi-no-Kami, and revered the great divine virtue of trees. === Amulets === Amulets designed with storks (red, green, and purple) are well known as charms for fertility and safe childbirth (based on the tradition that storks bring children). In the fallow season (mainly September to May), storks fly in to the adjacent wetland. ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Torii001.jpg|Approach entrance (stork images are painted on the railings of the sacred bridge over the Shimonomiya River) ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Sintoism002.jpg|Wari-haiden ファイル:Kukuhi-jinja_0383.JPG|Stork-designed amulets === Access === * About 10 minutes on foot from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=KTR|1=UNKNOWN}}{{ill|lt=Kōnotori-no-Sato Station|Q7676863|WD=Q7676863|1=Kōnotori-no-sato Station}}. * From {{ill|lt=San'in Main Line|Q1135170|WD=Q1135170|1=San'in Main Line}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka Station (Hyogo)|Q871612|WD=Q871612|lt=Toyooka Station|1=Toyooka Station (Hyōgo)}}, take {{ill|lt=Zentan Bus|Q11389110|WD=Q11389110|1=Zentan Bus}} bound for Shimonomiya and get off at "Shimonomiya-guchi". === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Related items === === External links === {{Commonscat|Kukuhi-jinja}} {{Osm box|n|5597854784}} * [http://www.city.toyooka.lg.jp/kanko/miru/1002163.html Facility Guide: Kukuhi Shrine] - Toyooka City {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くくひしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:兵庫県の神社]] [[Category:但馬国の式内社]] [[Category:兵庫県の旧村社]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]] [[Category:兵庫県の重要文化財]] [[Category:室町時代の建築]] [[Category:豊岡市の建築物]] [[Category:豊岡市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qqq]]

Built in Eishō 4 (1507), Muromachi period. === Stork legend === According to the {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}, on the first day of the tenth month in the 23rd year of Emperor {{ill|lt=Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}, while the emperor stood before the palace with Prince '''Homutsuwake''' (誉津別皇子), a '''kugui''' (an old word for {{ill|lt=stork|Q998495|WD=Q998495|1=Oriental stork}}) cried as it crossed the sky. '''{{ill|lt=Amenoyukawa no Tana|Q52319907|WD=Q52319907|1=Ame-no-Yukawata}}''' said, "I will surely capture and present it," and pursued the great bird through many provinces; it is said he captured it in {{ill|lt=Izumo Province|Q582846|WD=Q582846|1=Izumo Province}}, or according to another tradition, in {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727|1=Tajima Province}}. On the first day of the eleventh month, he successfully presented the bird. At that time the prince was thirty years old, but had still not spoken and only made sounds like a crying child; on that day he spoke human words for the first time. Because the kugui was such a sacred bird, its nesting place was called Kukuhi. Later, a shrine was built there and the tree deity '''Kukunochi-no-Kami''' was enshrined. This is said to be the beginning of Kukuhi Shrine. At that time, however, the '''Toyooka Basin''' was called "Kimesaki no Umi" and was entirely an inlet of the sea; Shimonomiya stood on the shoreline of that inlet. The area was also densely wooded, a place where {{ill|lt=kodama|Q1515938|WD=Q1515938|1=Kodama (spirit)}} were thought to dwell, and a scenic site where kami themselves were believed to reside. It is therefore natural that earlier people perceived the mystery and sacredness of this nature, worshipped the tree deity Kukunochi-no-Kami, and revered the great divine virtue of trees. === Amulets === Amulets designed with storks (red, green, and purple) are well known as charms for fertility and safe childbirth (based on the tradition that storks bring children). In the fallow season (mainly September to May), storks fly in to the adjacent wetland. ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Torii001.jpg|Approach entrance (stork images are painted on the railings of the sacred bridge over the Shimonomiya River) ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Sintoism002.jpg|Wari-haiden ファイル:Kukuhi-jinja_0383.JPG|Stork-designed amulets === Access === * About 10 minutes on foot from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=KTR|1=UNKNOWN}}{{ill|lt=Kōnotori-no-Sato Station|Q7676863|WD=Q7676863|1=Kōnotori-no-sato Station}}. * From {{ill|lt=San'in Main Line|Q1135170|WD=Q1135170|1=San'in Main Line}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka Station (Hyogo)|Q871612|WD=Q871612|lt=Toyooka Station|1=Toyooka Station (Hyōgo)}}, take {{ill|lt=Zentan Bus|Q11389110|WD=Q11389110|1=Zentan Bus}} bound for Shimonomiya and get off at "Shimonomiya-guchi". === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Related items === === External links === {{Commonscat|Kukuhi-jinja}} {{Osm box|n|5597854784}} * [http://www.city.toyooka.lg.jp/kanko/miru/1002163.html Facility Guide: Kukuhi Shrine] - Toyooka City {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くくひしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:兵庫県の神社]] [[Category:但馬国の式内社]] [[Category:兵庫県の旧村社]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]] [[Category:兵庫県の重要文化財]] [[Category:室町時代の建築]] [[Category:豊岡市の建築物]] [[Category:豊岡市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qqq]]

Haiden |所在地={{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}Shimonomiya 318-2 |位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kukunochi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} |創建=Unknown (reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}) |社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |例祭={{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}} |神事= |本殿=Three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |地図= {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Kukuhi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Kukuhi-jinja 04230.JPG|thumb|250px|Honden (Important Cultural Property)]] '''Kukuhi Shrine''' (くくひじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Shimonomiya, {{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinai small shrine|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former rank under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was village shrine. === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kukunochi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} (くくのちのかみ) === History === The {{ill|lt=honden|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}} is a three-bay nagare-zukuri structure with a kokera-buki roof. It was built in {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1507|Q6161|WD=Q6161|1=1507}}) in the {{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|WD=Q334845|1=Muromachi period}}, and is designated a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}. Edo-period records describe it as an old shrine also called Munakata Daimyōjin, and there is also a theory that it enshrined {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tagiribime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Property (national designation) === * Honden - three-bay nagare-zukuri, kokera-buki. Built in Eishō 4 (1507), Muromachi period. === Stork legend === According to the {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}, on the first day of the tenth month in the 23rd year of Emperor {{ill|lt=Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}, while the emperor stood before the palace with Prince '''Homutsuwake''' (誉津別皇子), a '''kugui''' (an old word for {{ill|lt=stork|Q998495|WD=Q998495|1=Oriental stork}}) cried as it crossed the sky. When the prince asked, "What is that?", the emperor rejoiced greatly and asked his retainers who could capture the bird. '''{{ill|lt=Amenoyukawa no Tana|Q52319907|WD=Q52319907|1=Ame-no-Yukawata}}''' said, "I will surely capture and present it," and pursued the great bird through many provinces; it is said he captured it in {{ill|lt=Izumo Province|Q582846|WD=Q582846|1=Izumo Province}}, or according to another tradition, in {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727|1=Tajima Province}}. On the first day of the eleventh month, he successfully presented the bird. At that time the prince was thirty years old, but had still not spoken and only made sounds like a crying child; on that day he spoke human words for the first time. Because the kugui was such a sacred bird, its nesting place was called Kukuhi. Later, a shrine was built there and the tree deity '''Kukunochi-no-Kami''' was enshrined. This is said to be the beginning of Kukuhi Shrine. At that time, however, the '''Toyooka Basin''' was called "Kimesaki no Umi" and was entirely an inlet of the sea; Shimonomiya stood on the shoreline of that inlet. The area was also densely wooded, a place where {{ill|lt=kodama|Q1515938|WD=Q1515938|1=Kodama (spirit)}} were thought to dwell, and a scenic site where kami themselves were believed to reside. It is therefore natural that earlier people perceived the mystery and sacredness of this nature, worshipped the tree deity Kukunochi-no-Kami, and revered the great divine virtue of trees. === Amulets === Amulets designed with storks (red, green, and purple) are well known as charms for fertility and safe childbirth (based on the tradition that storks bring children). In the fallow season (mainly September to May), storks fly in to the adjacent wetland. ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Torii001.jpg|Approach entrance (stork images are painted on the railings of the sacred bridge over the Shimonomiya River) ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Sintoism002.jpg|Wari-haiden ファイル:Kukuhi-jinja_0383.JPG|Stork-designed amulets === Access === * About 10 minutes on foot from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=KTR|1=UNKNOWN}}{{ill|lt=Kōnotori-no-Sato Station|Q7676863|WD=Q7676863|1=Kōnotori-no-sato Station}}. * From {{ill|lt=San'in Main Line|Q1135170|WD=Q1135170|1=San'in Main Line}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka Station (Hyogo)|Q871612|WD=Q871612|lt=Toyooka Station|1=Toyooka Station (Hyōgo)}}, take {{ill|lt=Zentan Bus|Q11389110|WD=Q11389110|1=Zentan Bus}} bound for Shimonomiya and get off at "Shimonomiya-guchi". === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Related items === === External links === {{Commonscat|Kukuhi-jinja}} {{Osm box|n|5597854784}} * [http://www.city.toyooka.lg.jp/kanko/miru/1002163.html Facility Guide: Kukuhi Shrine] - Toyooka City {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くくひしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:兵庫県の神社]] [[Category:但馬国の式内社]] [[Category:兵庫県の旧村社]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]] [[Category:兵庫県の重要文化財]] [[Category:室町時代の建築]] [[Category:豊岡市の建築物]] [[Category:豊岡市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]]

It is a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinai small shrine|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former rank under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was village shrine. === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kukunochi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} (くくのちのかみ) === History === The {{ill|lt=honden|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}} is a three-bay nagare-zukuri structure with a kokera-buki roof. It was built in {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1507|Q6161|WD=Q6161|1=1507}}) in the {{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|WD=Q334845|1=Muromachi period}}, and is designated a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}. Edo-period records describe it as an old shrine also called Munakata Daimyōjin, and there is also a theory that it enshrined {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tagiribime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Property (national designation) === * Honden - three-bay nagare-zukuri, kokera-buki. Built in Eishō 4 (1507), Muromachi period. === Stork legend === According to the {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}, on the first day of the tenth month in the 23rd year of Emperor {{ill|lt=Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}, while the emperor stood before the palace with Prince '''Homutsuwake''' (誉津別皇子), a '''kugui''' (an old word for {{ill|lt=stork|Q998495|WD=Q998495|1=Oriental stork}}) cried as it crossed the sky. When the prince asked, "What is that?", the emperor rejoiced greatly and asked his retainers who could capture the bird. '''{{ill|lt=Amenoyukawa no Tana|Q52319907|WD=Q52319907|1=Ame-no-Yukawata}}''' said, "I will surely capture and present it," and pursued the great bird through many provinces; it is said he captured it in {{ill|lt=Izumo Province|Q582846|WD=Q582846|1=Izumo Province}}, or according to another tradition, in {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727|1=Tajima Province}}. On the first day of the eleventh month, he successfully presented the bird. At that time the prince was thirty years old, but had still not spoken and only made sounds like a crying child; on that day he spoke human words for the first time. Because the kugui was such a sacred bird, its nesting place was called Kukuhi. Later, a shrine was built there and the tree deity '''Kukunochi-no-Kami''' was enshrined. This is said to be the beginning of Kukuhi Shrine. At that time, however, the '''Toyooka Basin''' was called "Kimesaki no Umi" and was entirely an inlet of the sea; Shimonomiya stood on the shoreline of that inlet. The area was also densely wooded, a place where {{ill|lt=kodama|Q1515938|WD=Q1515938|1=Kodama (spirit)}} were thought to dwell, and a scenic site where kami themselves were believed to reside. It is therefore natural that earlier people perceived the mystery and sacredness of this nature, worshipped the tree deity Kukunochi-no-Kami, and revered the great divine virtue of trees. === Amulets === Amulets designed with storks (red, green, and purple) are well known as charms for fertility and safe childbirth (based on the tradition that storks bring children). In the fallow season (mainly September to May), storks fly in to the adjacent wetland. ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Torii001.jpg|Approach entrance (stork images are painted on the railings of the sacred bridge over the Shimonomiya River) ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Sintoism002.jpg|Wari-haiden ファイル:Kukuhi-jinja_0383.JPG|Stork-designed amulets === Access === * About 10 minutes on foot from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=KTR|1=UNKNOWN}}{{ill|lt=Kōnotori-no-Sato Station|Q7676863|WD=Q7676863|1=Kōnotori-no-sato Station}}. * From {{ill|lt=San'in Main Line|Q1135170|WD=Q1135170|1=San'in Main Line}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka Station (Hyogo)|Q871612|WD=Q871612|lt=Toyooka Station|1=Toyooka Station (Hyōgo)}}, take {{ill|lt=Zentan Bus|Q11389110|WD=Q11389110|1=Zentan Bus}} bound for Shimonomiya and get off at "Shimonomiya-guchi". === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Related items === === External links === {{Commonscat|Kukuhi-jinja}} {{Osm box|n|5597854784}} * [http://www.city.toyooka.lg.jp/kanko/miru/1002163.html Facility Guide: Kukuhi Shrine] - Toyooka City {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:兵庫県の神社]] [[Category:但馬国の式内社]] [[Category:兵庫県の旧村社]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]] [[Category:兵庫県の重要文化財]] [[Category:室町時代の建築]] [[Category:豊岡市の建築物]] [[Category:豊岡市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]]

category|Category:Kukuhi-jinja}} [[JA:久久比神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11368920}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=Kukuhi Shrine |画像=[[ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Honden Shintoism002.JPG|270px]]
Haiden |所在地={{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}Shimonomiya 318-2 |位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kukunochi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} |創建=Unknown (reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}) |社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |例祭={{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}} |神事= |本殿=Three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |地図= {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Kukuhi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Kukuhi-jinja 04230.JPG|thumb|250px|Honden (Important Cultural Property)]] '''Kukuhi Shrine''' (くくひじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Shimonomiya, {{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinai small shrine|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former rank under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was village shrine. === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kukunochi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} (くくのちのかみ) === History === The {{ill|lt=honden|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}} is a three-bay nagare-zukuri structure with a kokera-buki roof. It was built in {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1507|Q6161|WD=Q6161|1=1507}}) in the {{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|WD=Q334845|1=Muromachi period}}, and is designated a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}. Edo-period records describe it as an old shrine also called Munakata Daimyōjin, and there is also a theory that it enshrined {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tagiribime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Property (national designation) === * Honden - three-bay nagare-zukuri, kokera-buki. Built in Eishō 4 (1507), Muromachi period. === Stork legend === According to the {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}, on the first day of the tenth month in the 23rd year of Emperor {{ill|lt=Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}, while the emperor stood before the palace with Prince '''Homutsuwake''' (誉津別皇子), a '''kugui''' (an old word for {{ill|lt=stork|Q998495|WD=Q998495|1=Oriental stork}}) cried as it crossed the sky. When the prince asked, "What is that?", the emperor rejoiced greatly and asked his retainers who could capture the bird. '''{{ill|lt=Amenoyukawa no Tana|Q52319907|WD=Q52319907|1=Ame-no-Yukawata}}''' said, "I will surely capture and present it," and pursued the great bird through many provinces; it is said he captured it in {{ill|lt=Izumo Province|Q582846|WD=Q582846|1=Izumo Province}}, or according to another tradition, in {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727|1=Tajima Province}}. On the first day of the eleventh month, he successfully presented the bird. At that time the prince was thirty years old, but had still not spoken and only made sounds like a crying child; on that day he spoke human words for the first time. Because the kugui was such a sacred bird, its nesting place was called Kukuhi. Later, a shrine was built there and the tree deity '''Kukunochi-no-Kami''' was enshrined. This is said to be the beginning of Kukuhi Shrine. At that time, however, the '''Toyooka Basin''' was called "Kimesaki no Umi" and was entirely an inlet of the sea; Shimonomiya stood on the shoreline of that inlet. The area was also densely wooded, a place where {{ill|lt=kodama|Q1515938|WD=Q1515938|1=Kodama (spirit)}} were thought to dwell, and a scenic site where kami themselves were believed to reside. It is therefore natural that earlier people perceived the mystery and sacredness of this nature, worshipped the tree deity Kukunochi-no-Kami, and revered the great divine virtue of trees. === Amulets === Amulets designed with storks (red, green, and purple) are well known as charms for fertility and safe childbirth (based on the tradition that storks bring children). In the fallow season (mainly September to May), storks fly in to the adjacent wetland. ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Torii001.jpg|Approach entrance (stork images are painted on the railings of the sacred bridge over the Shimonomiya River) ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Sintoism002.jpg|Wari-haiden ファイル:Kukuhi-jinja_0383.JPG|Stork-designed amulets === Access === * About 10 minutes on foot from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=KTR|1=UNKNOWN}}{{ill|lt=Kōnotori-no-Sato Station|Q7676863|WD=Q7676863|1=Kōnotori-no-sato Station}}. * From {{ill|lt=San'in Main Line|Q1135170|WD=Q1135170|1=San'in Main Line}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka Station (Hyogo)|Q871612|WD=Q871612|lt=Toyooka Station|1=Toyooka Station (Hyōgo)}}, take {{ill|lt=Zentan Bus|Q11389110|WD=Q11389110|1=Zentan Bus}} bound for Shimonomiya and get off at "Shimonomiya-guchi". === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Related items === === External links === {{Commonscat|Kukuhi-jinja}} {{Osm box|n|5597854784}} * [http://www.city.toyooka.lg.jp/kanko/miru/1002163.html Facility Guide: Kukuhi Shrine] - Toyooka City {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:兵庫県の神社]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:Son-sha in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]] [[Category:兵庫県の重要文化財]] [[Category:室町時代の建築]] [[Category:豊岡市の建築物]] [[Category:豊岡市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]]

category|Category:Kukuhi-jinja}} [[JA:久久比神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11368920}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=Kukuhi Shrine |画像=[[ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Honden Shintoism002.JPG|270px]]
Haiden |所在地={{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}Shimonomiya 318-2 |位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kukunochi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} |創建=Unknown (reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}) |社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |例祭={{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}} |神事= |本殿=Three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |地図= {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Kukuhi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Kukuhi-jinja 04230.JPG|thumb|250px|Honden (Important Cultural Property)]] '''Kukuhi Shrine''' (くくひじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Shimonomiya, {{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinai small shrine|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former rank under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was village shrine. === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kukunochi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} (くくのちのかみ) === History === The {{ill|lt=honden|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}} is a three-bay nagare-zukuri structure with a kokera-buki roof. It was built in {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1507|Q6161|WD=Q6161|1=1507}}) in the {{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|WD=Q334845|1=Muromachi period}}, and is designated a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}. Edo-period records describe it as an old shrine also called Munakata Daimyōjin, and there is also a theory that it enshrined {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tagiribime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Property (national designation) === * Honden - three-bay nagare-zukuri, kokera-buki. Built in Eishō 4 (1507), Muromachi period. === Stork legend === According to the {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}, on the first day of the tenth month in the 23rd year of Emperor {{ill|lt=Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}, while the emperor stood before the palace with Prince '''Homutsuwake''' (誉津別皇子), a '''kugui''' (an old word for {{ill|lt=stork|Q998495|WD=Q998495|1=Oriental stork}}) cried as it crossed the sky. When the prince asked, "What is that?", the emperor rejoiced greatly and asked his retainers who could capture the bird. '''{{ill|lt=Amenoyukawa no Tana|Q52319907|WD=Q52319907|1=Ame-no-Yukawata}}''' said, "I will surely capture and present it," and pursued the great bird through many provinces; it is said he captured it in {{ill|lt=Izumo Province|Q582846|WD=Q582846|1=Izumo Province}}, or according to another tradition, in {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727|1=Tajima Province}}. On the first day of the eleventh month, he successfully presented the bird. At that time the prince was thirty years old, but had still not spoken and only made sounds like a crying child; on that day he spoke human words for the first time. Because the kugui was such a sacred bird, its nesting place was called Kukuhi. Later, a shrine was built there and the tree deity '''Kukunochi-no-Kami''' was enshrined. This is said to be the beginning of Kukuhi Shrine. At that time, however, the '''Toyooka Basin''' was called "Kimesaki no Umi" and was entirely an inlet of the sea; Shimonomiya stood on the shoreline of that inlet. The area was also densely wooded, a place where {{ill|lt=kodama|Q1515938|WD=Q1515938|1=Kodama (spirit)}} were thought to dwell, and a scenic site where kami themselves were believed to reside. It is therefore natural that earlier people perceived the mystery and sacredness of this nature, worshipped the tree deity Kukunochi-no-Kami, and revered the great divine virtue of trees. === Amulets === Amulets designed with storks (red, green, and purple) are well known as charms for fertility and safe childbirth (based on the tradition that storks bring children). In the fallow season (mainly September to May), storks fly in to the adjacent wetland. ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Torii001.jpg|Approach entrance (stork images are painted on the railings of the sacred bridge over the Shimonomiya River) ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Sintoism002.jpg|Wari-haiden ファイル:Kukuhi-jinja_0383.JPG|Stork-designed amulets === Access === * About 10 minutes on foot from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=KTR|1=UNKNOWN}}{{ill|lt=Kōnotori-no-Sato Station|Q7676863|WD=Q7676863|1=Kōnotori-no-sato Station}}. * From {{ill|lt=San'in Main Line|Q1135170|WD=Q1135170|1=San'in Main Line}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka Station (Hyogo)|Q871612|WD=Q871612|lt=Toyooka Station|1=Toyooka Station (Hyōgo)}}, take {{ill|lt=Zentan Bus|Q11389110|WD=Q11389110|1=Zentan Bus}} bound for Shimonomiya and get off at "Shimonomiya-guchi". === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Related items === === External links === {{Commonscat|Kukuhi-jinja}} {{Osm box|n|5597854784}} * [http://www.city.toyooka.lg.jp/kanko/miru/1002163.html Facility Guide: Kukuhi Shrine] - Toyooka City {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:Son-sha in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Hyogo prefecture]] [[Category:室町時代の建築]] [[Category:豊岡市の建築物]] [[Category:豊岡市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]]

category|Category:Kukuhi-jinja}} [[JA:久久比神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11368920}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=Kukuhi Shrine |画像=[[ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Honden Shintoism002.JPG|270px]]
Haiden |所在地={{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}Shimonomiya 318-2 |位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kukunochi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} |創建=Unknown (reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}) |社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |例祭={{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}} |神事= |本殿=Three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |地図= {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Kukuhi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Kukuhi-jinja 04230.JPG|thumb|250px|Honden (Important Cultural Property)]] '''Kukuhi Shrine''' (くくひじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Shimonomiya, {{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinai small shrine|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former rank under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was village shrine. === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kukunochi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} (くくのちのかみ) === History === The {{ill|lt=honden|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}} is a three-bay nagare-zukuri structure with a kokera-buki roof. It was built in {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1507|Q6161|WD=Q6161|1=1507}}) in the {{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|WD=Q334845|1=Muromachi period}}, and is designated a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}. Edo-period records describe it as an old shrine also called Munakata Daimyōjin, and there is also a theory that it enshrined {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tagiribime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Property (national designation) === * Honden - three-bay nagare-zukuri, kokera-buki. Built in Eishō 4 (1507), Muromachi period. === Stork legend === According to the {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}, on the first day of the tenth month in the 23rd year of Emperor {{ill|lt=Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}, while the emperor stood before the palace with Prince '''Homutsuwake''' (誉津別皇子), a '''kugui''' (an old word for {{ill|lt=stork|Q998495|WD=Q998495|1=Oriental stork}}) cried as it crossed the sky. When the prince asked, "What is that?", the emperor rejoiced greatly and asked his retainers who could capture the bird. '''{{ill|lt=Amenoyukawa no Tana|Q52319907|WD=Q52319907|1=Ame-no-Yukawata}}''' said, "I will surely capture and present it," and pursued the great bird through many provinces; it is said he captured it in {{ill|lt=Izumo Province|Q582846|WD=Q582846|1=Izumo Province}}, or according to another tradition, in {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727|1=Tajima Province}}. On the first day of the eleventh month, he successfully presented the bird. At that time the prince was thirty years old, but had still not spoken and only made sounds like a crying child; on that day he spoke human words for the first time. Because the kugui was such a sacred bird, its nesting place was called Kukuhi. Later, a shrine was built there and the tree deity '''Kukunochi-no-Kami''' was enshrined. This is said to be the beginning of Kukuhi Shrine. At that time, however, the '''Toyooka Basin''' was called "Kimesaki no Umi" and was entirely an inlet of the sea; Shimonomiya stood on the shoreline of that inlet. The area was also densely wooded, a place where {{ill|lt=kodama|Q1515938|WD=Q1515938|1=Kodama (spirit)}} were thought to dwell, and a scenic site where kami themselves were believed to reside. It is therefore natural that earlier people perceived the mystery and sacredness of this nature, worshipped the tree deity Kukunochi-no-Kami, and revered the great divine virtue of trees. === Amulets === Amulets designed with storks (red, green, and purple) are well known as charms for fertility and safe childbirth (based on the tradition that storks bring children). In the fallow season (mainly September to May), storks fly in to the adjacent wetland. ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Torii001.jpg|Approach entrance (stork images are painted on the railings of the sacred bridge over the Shimonomiya River) ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Sintoism002.jpg|Wari-haiden ファイル:Kukuhi-jinja_0383.JPG|Stork-designed amulets === Access === * About 10 minutes on foot from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=KTR|1=UNKNOWN}}{{ill|lt=Kōnotori-no-Sato Station|Q7676863|WD=Q7676863|1=Kōnotori-no-sato Station}}. * From {{ill|lt=San'in Main Line|Q1135170|WD=Q1135170|1=San'in Main Line}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka Station (Hyogo)|Q871612|WD=Q871612|lt=Toyooka Station|1=Toyooka Station (Hyōgo)}}, take {{ill|lt=Zentan Bus|Q11389110|WD=Q11389110|1=Zentan Bus}} bound for Shimonomiya and get off at "Shimonomiya-guchi". === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Related items === === External links === {{Commonscat|Kukuhi-jinja}} {{Osm box|n|5597854784}} * [http://www.city.toyooka.lg.jp/kanko/miru/1002163.html Facility Guide: Kukuhi Shrine] - Toyooka City {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:Son-sha in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Hyogo prefecture]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Buildings in Toyooka City]] [[Category:豊岡市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]]

It is therefore natural that earlier people perceived the mystery and sacredness of this nature, worshipped the tree deity Kukunochi-no-Kami, and revered the great divine virtue of trees. === Amulets === Amulets designed with storks (red, green, and purple) are well known as charms for fertility and safe childbirth (based on the tradition that storks bring children). In the fallow season (mainly September to May), storks fly in to the adjacent wetland. ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Torii001.jpg|Approach entrance (stork images are painted on the railings of the sacred bridge over the Shimonomiya River) ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Sintoism002.jpg|Wari-haiden ファイル:Kukuhi-jinja_0383.JPG|Stork-designed amulets === Access === * About 10 minutes on foot from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=KTR|1=UNKNOWN}}{{ill|lt=Kōnotori-no-Sato Station|Q7676863|WD=Q7676863|1=Kōnotori-no-sato Station}}. * From {{ill|lt=San'in Main Line|Q1135170|WD=Q1135170|1=San'in Main Line}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka Station (Hyogo)|Q871612|WD=Q871612|lt=Toyooka Station|1=Toyooka Station (Hyōgo)}}, take {{ill|lt=Zentan Bus|Q11389110|WD=Q11389110|1=Zentan Bus}} bound for Shimonomiya and get off at "Shimonomiya-guchi". === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Related items === === External links === {{Commonscat|Kukuhi-jinja}} {{Osm box|n|5597854784}} * [http://www.city.toyooka.lg.jp/kanko/miru/1002163.html Facility Guide: Kukuhi Shrine] - Toyooka City {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:Son-sha in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Hyogo prefecture]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Buildings in Toyooka City]] [[Category:History of Toyooka]] [[Category:pages]]

category|Category:Kukuhi-jinja}} [[JA:久久比神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11368920}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=Kukuhi Shrine |画像=[[ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Honden Shintoism002.JPG|270px]]
Haiden |所在地={{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}Shimonomiya 318-2 |位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kukunochi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} |創建=Unknown (reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}) |社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |例祭={{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}} |神事= |本殿=Three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |地図= {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Kukuhi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Kukuhi-jinja 04230.JPG|thumb|250px|Honden (Important Cultural Property)]] '''Kukuhi Shrine''' (くくひじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Shimonomiya, {{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinai small shrine|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former rank under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was village shrine. === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kukunochi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} (くくのちのかみ) === History === The {{ill|lt=honden|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}} is a three-bay nagare-zukuri structure with a kokera-buki roof. It was built in {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1507|Q6161|WD=Q6161|1=1507}}) in the {{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|WD=Q334845|1=Muromachi period}}, and is designated a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}. Edo-period records describe it as an old shrine also called Munakata Daimyōjin, and there is also a theory that it enshrined {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tagiribime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Property (national designation) === * Honden - three-bay nagare-zukuri, kokera-buki. Built in Eishō 4 (1507), Muromachi period. === Stork legend === According to the {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}, on the first day of the tenth month in the 23rd year of Emperor {{ill|lt=Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}, while the emperor stood before the palace with Prince '''Homutsuwake''' (誉津別皇子), a '''kugui''' (an old word for {{ill|lt=stork|Q998495|WD=Q998495|1=Oriental stork}}) cried as it crossed the sky. When the prince asked, "What is that?", the emperor rejoiced greatly and asked his retainers who could capture the bird. '''{{ill|lt=Amenoyukawa no Tana|Q52319907|WD=Q52319907|1=Ame-no-Yukawata}}''' said, "I will surely capture and present it," and pursued the great bird through many provinces; it is said he captured it in {{ill|lt=Izumo Province|Q582846|WD=Q582846|1=Izumo Province}}, or according to another tradition, in {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727|1=Tajima Province}}. On the first day of the eleventh month, he successfully presented the bird. At that time the prince was thirty years old, but had still not spoken and only made sounds like a crying child; on that day he spoke human words for the first time. Because the kugui was such a sacred bird, its nesting place was called Kukuhi. Later, a shrine was built there and the tree deity '''Kukunochi-no-Kami''' was enshrined. This is said to be the beginning of Kukuhi Shrine. At that time, however, the '''Toyooka Basin''' was called "Kimesaki no Umi" and was entirely an inlet of the sea; Shimonomiya stood on the shoreline of that inlet. The area was also densely wooded, a place where {{ill|lt=kodama|Q1515938|WD=Q1515938|1=Kodama (spirit)}} were thought to dwell, and a scenic site where kami themselves were believed to reside. It is therefore natural that earlier people perceived the mystery and sacredness of this nature, worshipped the tree deity Kukunochi-no-Kami, and revered the great divine virtue of trees. === Amulets === Amulets designed with storks (red, green, and purple) are well known as charms for fertility and safe childbirth (based on the tradition that storks bring children). In the fallow season (mainly September to May), storks fly in to the adjacent wetland. ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Torii001.jpg|Approach entrance (stork images are painted on the railings of the sacred bridge over the Shimonomiya River) ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Sintoism002.jpg|Wari-haiden ファイル:Kukuhi-jinja_0383.JPG|Stork-designed amulets === Access === * About 10 minutes on foot from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=KTR|1=UNKNOWN}}{{ill|lt=Kōnotori-no-Sato Station|Q7676863|WD=Q7676863|1=Kōnotori-no-sato Station}}. * From {{ill|lt=San'in Main Line|Q1135170|WD=Q1135170|1=San'in Main Line}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka Station (Hyogo)|Q871612|WD=Q871612|lt=Toyooka Station|1=Toyooka Station (Hyōgo)}}, take {{ill|lt=Zentan Bus|Q11389110|WD=Q11389110|1=Zentan Bus}} bound for Shimonomiya and get off at "Shimonomiya-guchi". === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Related items === === External links === {{Commonscat|Kukuhi-jinja}} {{Osm box|n|5597854784}} * [http://www.city.toyooka.lg.jp/kanko/miru/1002163.html Facility Guide: Kukuhi Shrine] - Toyooka City {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:Son-sha in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Hyogo prefecture]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Buildings in Toyooka City]] [[Category:History of Toyooka]] [[Category:pages]]

Built in Eishō 4 (1507), Muromachi period. === Stork legend === According to the {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}, on the first day of the tenth month in the 23rd year of Emperor {{ill|lt=Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}, while the emperor stood before the palace with Prince '''Homutsuwake''' (誉津別皇子), a '''kugui''' (an old word for {{ill|lt=stork|Q998495|WD=Q998495|1=Oriental stork}}) cried as it crossed the sky. When the prince asked, "What is that?", the emperor rejoiced greatly and asked his retainers who could capture the bird. '''{{ill|lt=Amenoyukawa no Tana|Q52319907|WD=Q52319907|1=Ame-no-Yukawata}}''' said, "I will surely capture and present it," and pursued the great bird through many provinces; it is said he captured it in {{ill|lt=Izumo Province|Q582846|WD=Q582846|1=Izumo Province}}, or according to another tradition, in {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727|1=Tajima Province}}. On the first day of the eleventh month, he successfully presented the bird. At that time the prince was thirty years old, but had still not spoken and only made sounds like a crying child; on that day he spoke human words for the first time. Because the kugui was such a sacred bird, its nesting place was called Kukuhi. Later, a shrine was built there and the tree deity '''Kukunochi-no-Kami''' was enshrined. This is said to be the beginning of Kukuhi Shrine. At that time, however, the '''Toyooka Basin''' was called "Kimesaki no Umi" and was entirely an inlet of the sea; Shimonomiya stood on the shoreline of that inlet. The area was also densely wooded, a place where {{ill|lt=kodama|Q1515938|WD=Q1515938|1=Kodama (spirit)}} were thought to dwell, and a scenic site where kami themselves were believed to reside. It is therefore natural that earlier people perceived the mystery and sacredness of this nature, worshipped the tree deity Kukunochi-no-Kami, and revered the great divine virtue of trees. === Amulets === Amulets designed with storks (red, green, and purple) are well known as charms for fertility and safe childbirth (based on the tradition that storks bring children). In the fallow season (mainly September to May), storks fly in to the adjacent wetland. ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Torii001.jpg|Approach entrance (stork images are painted on the railings of the sacred bridge over the Shimonomiya River) ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Sintoism002.jpg|Wari-haiden ファイル:Kukuhi-jinja_0383.JPG|Stork-designed amulets === Access === * About 10 minutes on foot from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=KTR|1=UNKNOWN}}{{ill|lt=Kōnotori-no-Sato Station|Q7676863|WD=Q7676863|1=Kōnotori-no-sato Station}}. * From {{ill|lt=San'in Main Line|Q1135170|WD=Q1135170|1=San'in Main Line}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka Station (Hyogo)|Q871612|WD=Q871612|lt=Toyooka Station|1=Toyooka Station (Hyōgo)}}, take {{ill|lt=Zentan Bus|Q11389110|WD=Q11389110|1=Zentan Bus}} bound for Shimonomiya and get off at "Shimonomiya-guchi". === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Related items === === External links === {{Commonscat|Kukuhi-jinja}} {{Osm box|n|5597854784}} * [http://www.city.toyooka.lg.jp/kanko/miru/1002163.html Facility Guide: Kukuhi Shrine] - Toyooka City {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:Son-sha in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Hyogo prefecture]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Buildings in Toyooka City]] [[Category:History of Toyooka]]

former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |例祭={{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}} |神事= |本殿=Three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |地図= {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Kukuhi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Kukuhi-jinja 04230.JPG|thumb|250px|Honden (Important Cultural Property)]] '''Kukuhi Shrine''' (くくひじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Shimonomiya, {{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinai small shrine|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former rank under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was village shrine. === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kukunochi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} (くくのちのかみ) === History === The {{ill|lt=honden|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}} is a three-bay nagare-zukuri structure with a kokera-buki roof. It was built in {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1507|Q6161|WD=Q6161|1=1507}}) in the {{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|WD=Q334845|1=Muromachi period}}, and is designated a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}. Edo-period records describe it as an old shrine also called Munakata Daimyōjin, and there is also a theory that it enshrined {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tagiribime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Cultural properties === === Important Cultural Property (national designation) === * Honden - three-bay nagare-zukuri, kokera-buki. Built in Eishō 4 (1507), Muromachi period. === Stork legend === According to the {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}, on the first day of the tenth month in the 23rd year of Emperor {{ill|lt=Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}, while the emperor stood before the palace with Prince '''Homutsuwake''' (誉津別皇子), a '''kugui''' (an old word for {{ill|lt=stork|Q998495|WD=Q998495|1=Oriental stork}}) cried as it crossed the sky. When the prince asked, "What is that?", the emperor rejoiced greatly and asked his retainers who could capture the bird. '''{{ill|lt=Amenoyukawa no Tana|Q52319907|WD=Q52319907|1=Ame-no-Yukawata}}''' said, "I will surely capture and present it," and pursued the great bird through many provinces; it is said he captured it in {{ill|lt=Izumo Province|Q582846|WD=Q582846|1=Izumo Province}}, or according to another tradition, in {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727|1=Tajima Province}}. On the first day of the eleventh month, he successfully presented the bird. At that time the prince was thirty years old, but had still not spoken and only made sounds like a crying child; on that day he spoke human words for the first time. Because the kugui was such a sacred bird, its nesting place was called Kukuhi. Later, a shrine was built there and the tree deity '''Kukunochi-no-Kami''' was enshrined. This is said to be the beginning of Kukuhi Shrine. At that time, however, the '''Toyooka Basin''' was called "Kimesaki no Umi" and was entirely an inlet of the sea; Shimonomiya stood on the shoreline of that inlet. The area was also densely wooded, a place where {{ill|lt=kodama|Q1515938|WD=Q1515938|1=Kodama (spirit)}} were thought to dwell, and a scenic site where kami themselves were believed to reside. It is therefore natural that earlier people perceived the mystery and sacredness of this nature, worshipped the tree deity Kukunochi-no-Kami, and revered the great divine virtue of trees. === Amulets === Amulets designed with storks (red, green, and purple) are well known as charms for fertility and safe childbirth (based on the tradition that storks bring children). In the fallow season (mainly September to May), storks fly in to the adjacent wetland. ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Torii001.jpg|Approach entrance (stork images are painted on the railings of the sacred bridge over the Shimonomiya River) ファイル:Kukuhi-Jinjya Sintoism002.jpg|Wari-haiden ファイル:Kukuhi-jinja_0383.JPG|Stork-designed amulets === Access === * About 10 minutes on foot from {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=KTR|1=UNKNOWN}}{{ill|lt=Kōnotori-no-Sato Station|Q7676863|WD=Q7676863|1=Kōnotori-no-sato Station}}. * From {{ill|lt=San'in Main Line|Q1135170|WD=Q1135170|1=San'in Main Line}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka Station (Hyogo)|Q871612|WD=Q871612|lt=Toyooka Station|1=Toyooka Station (Hyōgo)}}, take {{ill|lt=Zentan Bus|Q11389110|WD=Q11389110|1=Zentan Bus}} bound for Shimonomiya and get off at "Shimonomiya-guchi". === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Related items === === External links === {{Commonscat|Kukuhi-jinja}} {{Osm box|n|5597854784}} * [http://www.city.toyooka.lg.jp/kanko/miru/1002163.html Facility Guide: Kukuhi Shrine] - Toyooka City {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kukuhi Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tajima Province]] [[Category:Son-sha in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Hyogo prefecture]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Toyooka City]] [[Category:History of Toyooka]]
